FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (2016-2021), 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: growth mixture modeling applied to ABCD longitudinal screen use data across 5 waves to identify trajectory subgroups.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Preventive Medicine Reports; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own separate numeric estimate anywhere in the paper. All analyses use a single composite total screen use variable. Step 2: The composite is defined as 'the sum of self-reported hours of daily use of television, videos, video games, social media, video chat, and texting' (Methods). TV, videos, and video games are non-phone/non-SM ingredients bundled in with no per-modality breakout. Composite fails Step 2.

notes: Screen use composite = TV + videos + video games + social media + video chat + texting; non-phone items bundled with no breakout. No per-modality estimates reported anywhere. Outcomes examined (sociodemographic characteristics, perceived racism/discrimination) are also demographic/contextual, which would independently fail IC2's other-side requirement.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study participants: N = 11,876 at baseline, 22 U.S. sites, ages 9-10, ABCD 4.0 release tabulated data (2016-2018 baseline, 1-year and 2-year follow-up).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: K-means clustering to identify SMA patterns, linear mixed effects modeling for cross-sectional and longitudinal associations with behavioral developmental measures and resting-state brain functional connectivity.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAAC; note mentions prior preprint posting but this is the published peer-reviewed version with no indication of a separate already-included peer-reviewed paper in this corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific variable has its own separate numeric estimate anywhere. All results are expressed as SMA subgroup comparisons (binary dummy: subgroup 1 vs subgroup 2). Step 2: The SMA composite is measured via the 14-item ABCD STQ, which includes 'watching TV or movies, playing video games' alongside social network sites, video chat, texting, and frequency of mature-rated games and movies (Methods, Variables section, Screen Media Activity). Non-phone/non-SM items (TV/movies, video games) are bundled into the clustering without a separate per-modality breakout in results. Composite fails Step 2.

notes: SMA pattern (higher-frequency vs lower-frequency) is IV; outcomes are neurocognitive performance (beta = -0.12), behavioral problems (beta = 0.13), psychotic-like experiences (beta = 0.31), RSFC — all individual-trait outcomes. IC2 fails only because the SMA pattern variable is derived from a composite including TV and video games with no per-modality breakout, not because of the other-side rule.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study, 21 U.S. sites, baseline ages 9-10, ABCD data curated release version 4.0 (https://nda.nih.gov; DOI 10.15154/1,523,041).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-validated machine learning models (PCR and LASSO) to predict ADHD symptoms using neurocognitive, demographic, and child/family characteristics including screen time.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Translational Psychiatry; no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: 'Weekday screen time' and 'weekend screen time' appear as named predictor features with their own feature weights in PCR/LASSO models (Fig. 3, Fig. 4). However, these are described in Methods as 'Children's estimates of their time spent engaged in screen media on typical weekdays and weekend days ... obtained from the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire [refs 48, 49].' The ABCD STQ is a multi-activity composite including TV, video games, social media, video chat, and texting — not a phone/SM-specific variable. No per-modality breakout (social media alone, texting alone, phone time alone) is presented anywhere in the paper. Step 2: The screen time measure bundles TV, video games, and other non-phone activities; composite fails Step 2. No qualifying phone/SM-specific estimate found.

notes: Screen time is used as IV predicting ADHD symptoms (individual-trait outcome). The other-side requirement would be satisfied if a qualifying SM/phone variable were present. IC2 fails because the screen time variable is a multi-activity STQ composite bundling TV, video games, and non-phone activities with no per-modality breakout.
